So let me do the engineering for you, since clearly nobody on here will. The bottleneck was never can you make light go through a waveguide. It’s all fucking thermals which is downstream of packaging. Specifically, how do you get a photonic engine onto the same substrate as a switch ASIC or XPU without your yield falling off a cliff and your reliability failing. TSMC’s answer is CoWoS where they bolt everything onto one big monolithic silicon interposer. Cute, until you hit the reticle limit and start duct-taping interposers together (CoWoS-S, then -R, then -L, soon -PleaseStop). Every chiplet and HBM stack you add to that single interposer compounds your defect probability and one bad die leads to a five-figure package going into the dumpster. CoWoS is thermally retarded and the whole industry knows this and it’s why capacity “can’t expand” and Jensen is acting like a bouncer in the front of a club choosing who gets pass the velvet rope. There is ONLY one company that will make copackaged optics work and expand in the rack… it’s not Lumentum, it’s not Coherent, it’s fucking INTELLLL. Intel’s EMIB gets rid of a giant reticle limited interposer and replaces it with a tiny silicon bridge that does the high-density coupling locally, exactly where you need it. You localize the hard part and the thermals in one area and your yield is ridiculously high. Comparing EMIB & CoWoS is so funny cause EMIB is north of 95% yield with like 12 reticle size equivalent package while CoWoS falls off a cliff after 5.5 reticles it’s that bad…now imagine adding thermally sensitive photonics. People don’t know this but Intel has been doing silicon photonics in-house for ~25 years... In 2024 they showed an Optical I/O chiplet doing 2 Tbps bidirectionally at ~5 pJ/bit, with the PIC and EIC co-packaged right against the ASIC and it’s all because of EMIB. And even more critically than that, they’ve actually run the fiber-attach and reliability/test flow to JEDEC-grade standards already, which everyone hand-waves until their links flap in production. My prediction is clear: Intel will capture over 90% of the copackaged silicon photonics market in the next five years because there is NO ALTERNATIVE."}}}, "rest_id": "2063735227382677573", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "492023", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2063766017411887492", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16293", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
How this is positive for $MRVL according to Ai

The genuine read-through for Marvell is the platform validation. SK Hynix is codeveloping memory for NVIDIA Vera Rubin AI supercomputers, Vera CPUs, RTX Spark-powered PCs and Jetson Thor platforms . Marvell already has its own NVIDIA tie-in: back in March 2026, NVIDIA and Marvell announced a strategic partnership connecting Marvell to the NVIDIA AI factory ecosystem through NVLink Fusion, and NVIDIA invested $2 billion in Marvell . Marvell provides custom XPUs and NVLink Fusion–compatible scale-up networking, while NVIDIA provides the Vera CPU, ConnectX NICs, Bluefield DPUs, NVLink interconnect and Spectrum-X switches . So anything that accelerates the Vera Rubin / AI-factory buildout broadly supports demand for the custom silicon, optical interconnect, and networking content Marvell sells into those same racks.
Notably, Marvell’s own IP portfolio includes custom high-bandwidth memory (HBM), silicon photonics, optical IO, and compute fabric interfaces like PCIe Gen 7  — so a healthier, faster-scaling memory supply chain feeding NVIDIA platforms is a tailwind for the systems Marvell co-designs into.
The honest counterpoints: this is a memory-maker deal, and Marvell doesn’t make DRAM/HBM cells — it integrates custom HBM into XPUs, so there’s no direct revenue line here. If anything, the bearish framing is that NVIDIA continues to deepen first-party and Korean-memory relationships, and Marvell’s bigger swing factor remains its custom XPU design wins with hyperscalers, not NVIDIA’s memory sourcing. Marvell already guided strongly on its own — it raised its fiscal 2027 and 2028 revenue outlook on exceptional AI bookings across 800G/1.6T optics, Ethernet switches, scale-up optical, DCI modules, and custom XPU solutions  — so this announcement doesn’t change Marvell’s fundamentals.
Bottom line: mildly positive sentiment read-through (validates the AI-factory/Vera Rubin ramp Marvell is levered to)

text: The country is networked with 200 micron fiberoptic fibers, to move data around the country. I used to make that some years ago. The space station used a smaller micron, I want to say it was 120 micron though it was some years ago. Slot machines use 200 micron. Im not familiar with photonics though sounds fascinating. There are interesting properties regarding nanometers, what light is measured in, and a reverse scale, tHz. Once refined, think of them as reverse scales. 540nm = 540tHz. 360nm = 720tHz. Their balances do some interesting things and I feel will be the future of data processing though not how one may think. Happy to explain if there is interest though I’ll warn you, cryptographers can’t keep up and the average person has no interest. Personally, I think I could teach it to the average eight year old, people are just set in there ways. Once quantum computing becomes a threat, there will be no choice other then to accept it. Personally, Im not holding my breath…

text:
$CRDO - CREDO IS BUILDING THE WHOLE AI-DATACENTER CONNECTIVITY STACK, AND IT RIPPED 7% TODAY

Credo's fiscal-Q4 revenue hit $437M, up 157% year over year, as hyperscalers standardize on its ZeroFlap active electrical cables for power-efficient in-rack interconnect. The DustPhotonics acquisition extends Credo from copper AECs into silicon photonics across 800G, 1.6T and 3.2T, and a new Rebellions tie-up drops its cables straight into RebelPOD AI clusters.

The mechanism is vertical integration of the unglamorous layer AI cannot scale without: as GPU clusters grow, the bottleneck moves from compute to moving bits between racks at low power — and Credo now sells both the copper and the optics for that hop.

$IONQ the most impressive mover of the day, the stock is likely to be totally re-rated upon successful $SKYT acquisition

$XNDU still one of the best plays to get exposure to  photonics, quantum computing and PennyLane all in one company.

text:
🚨The AI chips are useless without it. 

A GPU is just expensive silicon until you can move data between them at the speed AI demands and that connection layer is becoming the real bottleneck.

That's the $CRDO thesis. And I've been long for a while.

$CRDO built its name on copper connectivity -> the SerDes, DSPs, and active electrical cables that move data between AI chips. The one open question was always what happens when the industry shifts from copper to optical. With the DustPhotonics acquisition, Credo brought silicon photonics in-house and now owns the full connectivity stack: SerDes → DSP → active cables → optical transceivers → silicon photonics, spanning 800G to a 3.2T roadmap. Electrical and optical, end to end.

That's the moat. When Jensen says the future of AI depends on connectivity, this is the layer he means and $CRDO is the pure-play that owns all of it.

The fundamentals: FY26 revenue more than tripled to $1.34B, non-GAAP net income rose 5x to $662M, ~68% gross margins, $1.4B cash and no debt.

$CRDO - run the forward math*👇

→ Revenue CAGR (FY26–FY28): ~61%
→ PEG: ~0.52 -> Re-Rate ✨
→ EV/Sales: ~17x FY27 → ~11x FY28
→ Forward P/E: ~65x trailing → ~42x FY27 → ~27x FY28

A company compounding revenue at ~61% with a PEG of ~0.5.

The multiple isn't the story. The growth is. Watch the "expensive" 65x compress as earnings catch up to 42x, then 27x.

“Expensive” on today's numbers. Cheap on where it's going + re-rate.

Everyone's buying the chips.

The chips are useless if you can't connect them.

$CRDO owns the connection. 👀

*Estimated from projections

text:
I've been looking into ZBLAN optical fiber as it's one of the clearer cases for manufacturing in space. 

ZBLAN is a heavy metal fluoride glass fiber. It offers the potential for lower optical loss than conventional silica fiber and transmits across a broader wavelength range into the mid-infrared. These traits could support longer transmission distances and new uses in sensing and communications.

On Earth, producing high-quality ZBLAN has been difficult. Gravity induces convection in the molten glass during the drawing process leads to microscopic crystals and defects that scatter light and limit performance.

In microgravity, these issues are reduced. The glass stays more homogeneous during drawing. Recent work by Flawless Photonics on the International Space Station produced over 11 kilometers of fiber in about a month.

The returned samples are under analysis, and microscope comparisons show much cleaner microstructures than Earth-produced fiber.

Challenges remain. ZBLAN is more brittle than silica and has a larger minimum bend radius, which affects handling. The technology is still in the demonstration phase but commercial production is being planned,and the plan is to manufacture it in space.

ZBLAN is a heavy metal fluoride glass fiber. It offers the potential for lower optical loss than conventional silica fiber and transmits across a broader wavelength range into the mid-infrared. These traits could support longer transmission distances and new uses in sensing and communications. On Earth, producing high-quality ZBLAN has been difficult. Gravity induces convection in the molten glass during the drawing process leads to microscopic crystals and defects that scatter light and limit performance. In microgravity, these issues are reduced. The glass stays more homogeneous during drawing. Recent work by Flawless Photonics on the International Space Station produced over 11 kilometers of fiber in about a month. The returned samples are under analysis, and microscope comparisons show much cleaner microstructures than Earth-produced fiber. Challenges remain. ZBLAN is more brittle than silica and has a larger minimum bend radius, which affects handling. The technology is still in the demonstration phase but commercial production is being planned,and the plan is to manufacture it in space."}}}, "rest_id": "2062650997277954293", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "57", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🚨 Scientists shrank a powerful femtosecond laser onto a chip.

This is not a laser pointer upgrade. It is a photonics platform breakthrough.

Ultrafast lasers produce extremely short pulses of light, often used for precision measurement, spectroscopy, medical imaging, atomic clocks, and chemical analysis.

The problem:

Traditional ultrafast laser systems can be bulky and complex.

In a Nature paper, researchers demonstrated an integrated mode-locked laser using an erbium-implanted silicon nitride photonic chip and a Mamyshev oscillator design.

The key result:
The chip-scale laser produced nanojoule-level pulses, could be compressed to 147 femtoseconds, and directly drove a broad supercontinuum without extra amplification.

Why it matters:
This could help move powerful ultrafast laser tools from large lab systems toward compact, portable devices.

Important nuance:
Not a consumer product yet.
Not replacing every laser system tomorrow.

Still advanced photonics research.

Translation:
Real chip-scale laser milestone. Smaller tools.
Big potential for sensing, spectroscopy, medical diagnostics, and precision timing.

Source:
High-pulse-energy integrated mode-locked laser using a Mamyshev oscillator. Nature. 2026.
https://t.co/TE9fxK75U4

This is not a laser pointer upgrade. It is a photonics platform breakthrough. Ultrafast lasers produce extremely short pulses of light, often used for precision measurement, spectroscopy, medical imaging, atomic clocks, and chemical analysis. The problem: Traditional ultrafast laser systems can be bulky and complex. In a Nature paper, researchers demonstrated an integrated mode-locked laser using an erbium-implanted silicon nitride photonic chip and a Mamyshev oscillator design. The key result: The chip-scale laser produced nanojoule-level pulses, could be compressed to 147 femtoseconds, and directly drove a broad supercontinuum without extra amplification. Why it matters: This could help move powerful ultrafast laser tools from large lab systems toward compact, portable devices. Important nuance: Not a consumer product yet. Not replacing every laser system tomorrow. Still advanced photonics research. Translation: Real chip-scale laser milestone. Smaller tools. Big potential for sensing, spectroscopy, medical diagnostics, and precision timing. Source: High-pulse-energy integrated mode-locked laser using a Mamyshev oscillator. That's not an opinion, it's physics. Every data center being built right now is replacing electrical connections with light. NVIDIA confirmed it with $4.5 billion in direct investment. I mapped 25 public companies across the photonics value chain: Every AI cluster being built today hits the same wall. A hundred thousand GPUs mean nothing if the data can't move between them fast enough. Copper maxed out years ago and photonics replaced it: lasers, optical fiber, and transceivers that push data at the speed of light. The AI transceiver market doubled in two years. NVIDIA committed $4.5 billion across three photonics companies this year alone. This is where the infrastructure money is going. Here's the full value chain: 🔬 MATERIALS & WAFERS This is the bottom of the chain. Every laser and transceiver starts as a wafer substrate: indium phosphide, gallium arsenide, germanium, specialty glass. Nobody above this layer can produce anything without these inputs, and right now the most critical one, indium phosphide, is the tightest material in the entire AI supply chain. The gap between demand and capacity is getting worse, not better. I think this is the most asymmetric layer on the map. Investors chase the transceiver companies and ignore who grows the substrates underneath them. But NVIDIA is writing checks worth billions in cash and warrants to lock up supply from this exact layer. First link in the chain, last to get attention, and the one that chokes everything above it if it breaks. Tickers: $GLW, $AXTI, $IQE, $AIXA, $AMS 💡 CORE PHOTONIC DEVICES This layer converts electricity into light and back. Without it, zero data moves through fiber. NVIDIA dropped $4 billion into two companies here this year just to secure production capacity, and both of them joined the S&P 500 within weeks of each other. That should tell you how fast this went from niche to essential. The supply gap is not closing. The companies shipping next gen lasers at volume can be counted on one hand, and switching suppliers takes years of requalification. Order books stretch past twelve months. Every next generation GPU cluster consumes more of these components than the last, and no one can substitute them on short notice. I watch this layer more closely than any other. Tickers: $IPGP, $COHR, $LITE, $LASR, $SIVE 🔌 COMPONENTS & MODULES The companies here take raw lasers and detectors, package them into finished transceivers and modules, and ship them straight to hyperscalers. If the layers below are the engine, this is the vehicle that actually reaches the customer. Hyperscaler purchase orders land here. The revenue acceleration shows up here first. What I like about this layer is that you can underwrite it today, not in two years. These are businesses with signed capacity commitments and product already moving. The consolidation angle matters too: larger photonics players have already started absorbing standalone module companies, and whoever remains independent gains pricing power as options thin out. Tickers: $AAOI, $MTSI, $VIAV, $LPTH ⚙️ SYSTEMS & EQUIPMENT No company above this layer can manufacture a single photonic component without the machines built here. One of these names holds 100% of the EUV lithography market with zero competitors. Others supply the bonding equipment for co packaged optics or the process control instruments used across the majority of advanced packaging lines. If photonics is the gold rush, this is the layer selling the picks. My honest take: this is where the smart, patient capital parks. Equipment companies have pricing power and multi year order books that generate cash through full capex cycles. They attract holders who don't panic on the first pullback. The stocks don't run 1,000% overnight, but they compound while everything above them swings, and that tradeoff is worth more than most people give it credit for. Tickers: $ASML, $BESI, $ASM, $LPKF, $MKSI 🔍 TEST, METROLOGY & YIELD The most ignored layer on this map, and arguably the one with the cleanest business model. Every wafer, laser, and transceiver has to be tested and verified before it ships. As speeds climb and photonic devices get more complex, the testing challenge compounds fast. The industry is now constrained not only by what it can build but by what it can prove actually works. Yield is money. Better defect detection means better margins for every company upstream, which is why foundries keep buying test equipment even when they slash budgets everywhere else. These are capital light businesses tied to every unit of production across the chain. Last check before product hits the customer, and one of the few layers where demand doesn't cycle down when the rest of semis softens. Tickers: $CAMT, $FORM, $AEHR, $ONTO, $VIAV 🧠FINAL THOUGHTS The NVIDIA capital concentration tells the whole story. One company wrote $4.5 billion in checks to three photonics suppliers in a single quarter. That is a company locking down the one input that could bottleneck its GPU deployments: the optical interconnect. Returns across this sector have been historic over the past twelve months. But separate the revenue growers from the narrative trades. Some of these companies are printing real quarterly numbers that would impress in any sector. Others are carrying multi billion dollar market caps on sub $100 million in annual revenue. Same sector, wildly different risk. Every generation of AI infrastructure from here forward needs more photonics. Not less. The copper to light transition inside data centers is early. Co packaged optics is barely in deployment, and 1.6T transceivers are ramping with 3.2T already on roadmaps. The chain locks together: stress on any single link reprices every link above it."}}}, "rest_id": "2062502220889837836", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "267095", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2062636370233831773", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1096", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
In addition to the smart ring, new ATMs and payment terminals, and a live stand, Sber presented several other new developments at the SPIEF.

One of these is GigaNetwork. This is a platform where AI agents autonomously perform routine business processes. They search for counterparties, conduct tenders, and support transactions at all stages.

An optical computer for AI tasks was also presented. This is a new type of device: it uses light, not electronics, for calculations. According to the developers, the transition to photonics will reduce the costs of training and using large models. This is a real way to build an efficient computing infrastructure.

text:
Is it weird to say I still feel bullish despite the massive collapse today? 6/9 stocks in my port are down over 10% today, with SK hynix taking the questionable throne, dropping a staggering 18.6%. This is the worst day for my port in 2026 so far.

Despite that, I still feel bullish, because $META is reportedly planning an equity issuance worth $10's of billions of dollars (per FT), following $GOOG's equity issuance earlier this week. This would mean even more CapEx spending, thus even more spending on memory and photonics. 

That is bullish for $MU, $SNDK, SK hynix and $AAOI in my port. For now, the charts look extremely ugly. Longer term, I remain bullish.

text:
$LITE $COHR $AAOI 

I’m obviously a huge Nvidia bull.

I’m a huge quantum bull.

I’m a huge memory bull.

And I’m a huge physical AI / Tesla bull.

Having said that, there is one category that stands out as potentially offering the best generational buying opportunity b/t now and the end of this correction. It is AI networking / optical (photonics) interconnects.

This category is poised to face a tighter supply bottleneck than chips and even memory during the next phase of AI infrastructure buildout. Personally, I’m already building my photonics portfolio w/ a 2 year holding horizon. Note that this is distinct from my quantum positions which I plan to hold for many more years.

text:
🚨 CONFIRMED via Finansinspektionen (Swedish FSA): JPMorgan Chase & Co. just crossed the 5% ownership threshold in $SIVE.

Official filing:
→ Holder: JPMorgan Chase & Co.
→ Transaction date: June 2, 2026 (same day as the GlobalFoundries announcement)
→ Reason for flagging: "Köp" (BUY)
→ Pre-transaction position: 0 shares
→ Post-transaction position: 16,098,583 shares / 5.25008% of total voting rights

Position structure:
→ J.P. Morgan Securities plc (UK entity): 15,916,852 voting rights — 5.19%
→ J.P. Morgan Securities LLC (US entity): 181,731 voting rights — 0.06%
→ Direct shares: 10,049,558 (3.14% of total shares)
→ Cash-settled instruments (likely swaps): 6,049,025 share equivalents

Why this is structurally significant:

Swedish law requires mandatory disclosure when crossing the 5% ownership threshold. JPMorgan went from ZERO to 5.25% on June 2. The flagging reason filed with the FSA was explicitly "Köp" (Buy) — not custody, not market-making inventory. This is a deliberate institutional position.

The timing is what makes it remarkable:

June 2 was the same day GlobalFoundries announced the silicon photonics reference design partnership and $SIVE moved +50-70%.

Three possible reads:
JPM was positioned beforehand and captured the move

JPM built the position aggressively into and after the news.

JPM is positioning ahead of the Nasdaq NY listing in an underwriter / placement agent role

All three are bullish. The third would be the most strategically significant — tier-1 US investment banks don't take 5% positions in Swedish micro-caps without strategic context.

Connecting back to the "zero institutional ownership" post earlier this week:

Five days ago: $SIVE had zero US institutional 13F filings.

Today: the world's largest investment bank discloses a 5%+ position triggered by a "Buy" transaction.

The institutional layer that was missing has started forming.
The composition tells you it's sophisticated:
6 million of the 16 million share-equivalents are in cash-settled derivatives. The rest is direct shares. This is the kind of structured position you set up when you're a major capital markets player preparing for a multi-event sequence — index inclusions, dual listing, sector rotation.

JPMorgan doesn't take 5% positions in $2-3B Swedish micro-caps because they're bored.

$SIVE just got its first major Wall Street institutional anchor.

$SIVE

text:
5/6
Hybrid quantum classical systems are where the magic happens. No single platform wins alone. We’re seeing:
• Neutral atoms for scalable logic
• Photonics for networking & sensing
• Cryo-CMOS + magnons for compact control/memory
• AI agents running microsecond corrections (like in fusion)  This multi modal stack is accelerating the roadmap toward real advantage in materials, optimization, chemistry, and beyond years faster than single tech predictions.

text:
Quantum computing just went multi-platform and it’s mind blowing.  2026 isn’t about one winner taking all. Google just announced it’s adding neutral-atom systems alongside its superconducting roadmap. Companies are mixing neutral atoms, photonics, diamond spins, and more creating hybrid powerhouses.  
The era of “my qubits vs yours” is over. It’s now about smart combinations that actually deliver useful advantage. Let’s unpack why this feels like the real tipping point.

text:
Every dollar of TSMC's projected $500 billion chip-market upgrade depends on a technology the industry doesn't actually have yet at scale. The wiring inside today's AI chips physically can't carry tomorrow's workloads. Photonics is the only exit.

$TSM

text:
#VLSI2026 Highlight
NVIDIA's receiver sensitivity at photodiode is -17.3dBm & -18.9dBm at 32Gb/s & 28Gb/s, respectively. Efficiency at 32Gb/s is 0.484pJ/b. Receiver is 7nm FinFET CMOS IC stacked on 65nm silicon photonics IC via Cu-Cu hybrid bonding./Part 2
https://t.co/R6kDZLT6M1 https://t.co/TwLvcxJLTy

text:
Aggregate inference compute demand grows regardless of where inference runs (Jevons paradox), which is the core of the bull case for hardware. 

Inference is structurally more open than training: Less CUDA-locked, more price sensitive. Regardless, I think enterprise will forever rely on neoclouds to reduce hardware CAPEX + in-house teams, so overarching investment thesis (whether big AI or locally hosted) is still the same. 

Stacked silicon, Photonics, Power Grids etc as y'all have been doing DD.

text:
Sivers $SIVEF $SIVE just landed exactly the validation the thesis needed.

Its laser arrays are now integrated into $GFS ’s silicon photonics + SCALE co-packaged optics platform -> the reference designs the AI data center buildout is being built on.

This is the “light, not copper” shift made concrete. The lasers that move data inside the cluster don’t get the headlines. They get the design wins.

Still early innings but the chokepoint is real, and $SIVE just got named in it. 🔥
embedded_text:
Many people read the Sivers $SIVE/GlobalFoundries $GFS PR and see "AI optics."

I see something more: GF's SCALE platform is explicitly positioned as a CPO solution for OCI (Optical Scale-Up Initiative), whose founding members are: NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI https://t.co/ey0CeT74Wk

text:
$GFS - GLOBALFOUNDRIES CLOSED TWO AI DEALS IN ONE DAY — PROCESSOR IP AND SILICON-PHOTONICS LASERS

On Tuesday GlobalFoundries completed its acquisition of Synopsys' ARC processor-IP business — which, bolted onto MIPS, gives it a software-to-silicon stack aimed at physical AI — and separately announced a silicon-photonics partnership with Sivers to integrate laser arrays into its AI-data-center optical reference designs. Shares rose ~6%.

GF is repositioning from a commodity trailing-edge foundry into the connectivity and edge-AI layers — processor IP, photonics, and a U.S.-backed quantum foundry — where the multiple expansion lives, on top of the first dividend it declared last month.

text:
🧠 $CRDO (Credo) — one of the strongest AI networking stocks nobody talked about two years ago

If $NVDA is the brain of AI,

then $CRDO is becoming one of the companies that builds the nervous system.

The company focuses on:

high-speed interconnects
Active Electrical Cables (AECs)
optical DSPs
silicon photonics
SerDes technology

for hyperscale AI data centers. (Credo)

⚙️ Where CRDO sits in the AI stack

AI Stack:

🧠 Compute

$NVDA

$AMD
💾 Memory

$MU

🌐 Networking

$AVGO

$MRVL
$CRDO
🔦 Optics

$LITE

$COHR
CRDO sits directly in:

AI connectivity and data movement.

As AI clusters scale toward hundreds of thousands or even millions of GPUs, the networking layer becomes increasingly critical. (Credo)

🔥 Why institutions are excited

1️⃣ Explosive growth

Recent fiscal Q4 results were enormous:

Revenue up 157% YoY

EPS up 231% YoY

Sixth consecutive quarter of triple-digit revenue growth
Yet the stock sold off because expectations had become extremely high. (Barron's)
2️⃣ AEC leadership

Credo is widely viewed as the leader in:

Active Electrical Cables (AECs)

These products are increasingly used by hyperscalers because they:

consume less power

improve reliability

support higher bandwidth AI clusters. (Credo)

Major hyperscalers are already customers. (Barron's)
3️⃣ Moving aggressively into optics
This is the part many investors miss.

Credo recently acquired DustPhotonics to bring silicon photonics technology in-house, expanding into:

800G optics

1.6T optics

future CPO architectures

This significantly strengthens its position in the next generation of AI networking. (MarketWatch)

🧠 Why CRDO matters for the CPO theme
You recently asked about Bank of America's CPO report.
The most interesting names were:

AVGO

MRVL

LITE

COHR

I would add:

CRDO is becoming a sleeper CPO beneficiary.
Because the company now spans:
copper interconnects
optical DSPs

silicon photonics

near-package optical connectivity

Management increasingly describes the company as a connectivity platform spanning copper, optics, telemetry, and future architectures. (Seeking Alpha)

⚔️ CRDO vs peers
$AVGO
networking king
lower risk

larger company

$MRVL

switch ASICs + custom AI silicon

$LITE

optical components
$CRDO
fastest growth

connectivity specialist

highest AI networking leverage

⚠️ Risks

🧊 Valuation

The stock has already had a huge run.

Expectations are extremely high. (Barron's)
🧊 Customer concentration
Large hyperscalers drive a significant portion of demand. (Chronicle Journal Markets)

🧊 AI capex cycle

If hyperscaler spending slows:

networking names can correct sharply.

🧠 My take

Among the AI networking names, my ranking today is:

$AVGO — safest leader

$CRDO — highest growth

$MRVL — strongest upside optionality

$LITE — optics leader

If your thesis is:

AI clusters continue getting bigger,

then networking may become as important as GPUs.

And CRDO is one of the purest ways to play that trend. (Credo)

Bottom line
$CRDO is no longer just a cable company.
It is becoming:
an AI connectivity platform spanning AECs, optics, silicon photonics, and next-generation AI networking.

For investors looking beyond GPUs, CRDO is one of the most important names to watch in the AI data movement layer. (Credo)

Dr. Bastani is a veteran executive in the global semiconductor industry with over 40 years of operational and board-level experience. One of his current responsibilities includes:

Sivers Semiconductors AB: He joined as a Strategic Advisor to the Board and Management team in August 2023. In early 2024, he was appointed US Executive Chairman of Sivers Semiconductors Inc. and nominated as Chairman of the Board of Directors for the Swedish parent company, Sivers Semiconductors AB. His mandate focuses on accelerating the company's US growth strategy following its acquisition of Mixcomm (a mmWave 5G/Satcom specialist).

P.S:  2015 – 2023 Dr. Bastani worked for GlobalFoundries as the Senior VP & GM, Mobile & Wireless Infrastructure; later Senior Advisor to the CEO of GF

text: @USAFDoc3535 Im up 60% on $AAOX in the last two days because I realized that we are in greatest technological transformation since the industrial revolution and then full ported a double leveraged photonics ETF. Real nigga shit.

Many people read the Sivers $SIVE/GlobalFoundries $GFS PR and see "AI optics."

I see something more: GF's SCALE platform is explicitly positioned as a CPO solution for OCI (Optical Scale-Up Initiative), whose founding members are: NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI

Hennce, Sivers $SIVE just became the first publicly named laser partner integrated into SCALE reference designs. GF CPO solution for OCI. SCALE is the Industry First MSA capable CPO platform. 

OCI drives the transition from 800G EMLs to 1.6T/3.2T SiPh + CW laser architectures, this partnership hence is highly strategically important.

Not just a partnership announcement, it is an ecosystem announcement.

"Sivers’ laser arrays will also be available in GF’s Silicon Photonics Co-packaged Advanced Light Engine (SCALE™) platform for next-generation optical sub-assemblies and light engine architectures":

https://t.co/MKT1Lu4evA

text:
Let me explain what the GlobalFoundries deal actually does for $SIVE — because most retail is reading "partnership" and missing the structural significance.

Reference designs are how foundry ecosystems work.
When a chip designer wants to build silicon photonics (let's say AMD, Broadcom, or a hyperscaler ASIC team), they don't start from scratch. They start with the foundry's reference design — a pre-validated blueprint for how the components fit together.
GlobalFoundries' silicon photonics platform is one of the most strategically positioned in the industry. Their SCALE™ optical engine is the foundation for next-generation co-packaged optics.

$SIVE's lasers are now embedded in that reference design.

What this means in practice:

→ Every customer who designs silicon photonics on GF's platform starts with $SIVE lasers as the default light source
→ Switching to a non-$SIVE laser requires custom engineering work, custom validation, and supply chain risk
→ $SIVE has effectively become the path of least resistance for the entire GF silicon photonics customer base

The math of why this is different from a normal customer win:
A typical customer deal = one company buys $SIVE lasers.

A foundry reference design = every customer using that foundry's silicon photonics platform encounters $SIVE lasers in their default design flow.

One captures one customer. The other captures an ecosystem.

The competitive context:
Coherent, Lumentum, MACOM — these are the larger optical/photonics names retail compares $SIVE to. None of them just got embedded in a major foundry's silicon photonics reference design at the laser layer.

$SIVE just did something its bigger competitors did not.
And the addressable market GF is targeting? $25B in pluggable optics by 2030.

This is the moat people argued $SIVE didn't have. It just got built. $SIVE

"user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNjE5MjEwMDk0NTY4NjUyODA=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "$DBO.TO D-BOX Continues Growth Trajectory Surpassing 1,200 Active Screens and Generating Fourth Quarter Adjusted EBITDA of $3.2 Million Fueled by $3.0 Million of Royalties “D-BOX delivered exceptional fourth quarter and fiscal 2026 results, reflecting continued execution across our theatrical focused growth strategy” said Naveen Prasad, CEO of D-BOX. “During the year, we achieved a record expansion of our theatrical footprint by adding 189 net new screens, taking us to 1,201 active theatrical screens globally. This growth reflects increasing recognition from our exhibitor partners that D-BOX is a highly deployable premium experience that can help maximize per-patron yield by delivering a uniquely immersive experience for audiences of all ages. Our total revenues grew by 35% year-over-year to $57.6 million, while adjusted EBITDA more than doubled to $15.5 million. Net profit before income taxes surged 193% to $11.4 million. These robust results have further strengthened our balance sheet and enhanced our financial flexibility, positioning D-BOX to offer innovative financing solutions and capitalize on strategic opportunities as we move forward.” Q4 2026 Operating Results Fourth quarter royalty revenues increased 33% to $3.0 million compared with $2.2 million in the prior year. This year‑over‑year increase was driven primarily by a 24.8%1 increase in the North American domestic box office, and the 18.7% expansion in our installed footprint. For the second consecutive quarter, the gross domestic box office remained less concentrated across a broader range of films, reflective of the seasonality and composition of the release calendar during the period, rather than being dominated by blockbuster titles, which are typically more favorable for D-BOX. D-BOX-encoded movies themselves delivered strong results among the highest grossing titles of the quarter, including Project Hail Mary, Avatar: Fire and Ash and Hoppers. System sales to theatrical customers increased 682% to $7.8 million compared with $1.0 million last year. This increase reflects D-BOX’s emergence as a preferred capital allocation priority for it’s exhibitors partners looking to maximize per-patron yield. Seventy four new screens were installed in the quarter, offset by 8 deactivations, compared to 6 net new screens from the same period last year. While this surge highlights the D-BOX brand’s growing market penetration and the impact of high-performing blockbuster content, we continue to maintain a disciplined outlook regarding future hardware cycles. We expect system sales to normalize toward historical patterns of seasonality, as exhibitor capital expenditures typically fluctuate based on our exhibitor partners' positioning for the strength and timing of the global box office slate and their corresponding free cash flow generation. The Company ended the year with a significantly improved financial position: cash and cash equivalents increased to $17.6 million (up from $7.8 million), working capital rose to $24.7 million, and equity nearly doubled to $34.6 million. These improvements materially strengthen the Company's financial flexibility provide a solid foundation for continued investing in innovation, operational excellence, and strategic exhibitor financing and deployment initiatives. The strengthened balance sheet also supports disciplined capital allocation initiatives focused on creating long term shareholder value, including initiatives such as the Normal Course Issuer Bid (NCIB). Additionally, the Company recorded a deferred tax asset (DTA) of $6.4 million, relating to the recognition of previously unused tax losses and unused tax credits. This recognition is based on the expectation that future taxable profits will be available to utilize these losses and credits, supported by the Company’s recent history of success and positive outlook for future cash flows and profitability. Net profit for the quarter reached $1.9 million. Adjusted EBITDA2 totaled $3.2 million, representing a 22% Adjusted EBITDA margin², up 105% year-over-year and demonstrating continued focus on cost control and operational efficiency. Given the inherent variability and seasonality of quarterly sales, we continue to emphasize the importance of assessing the Company’s performance on a trailing twelve-month basis. FY26 Operating Results Total revenues increased by 35% year-over-year to $57.6 million, with adjusted EBITDA more than doubling to $15.5 million and net profit before income taxes rising 193% to $11.4 million. The Company’s strong top-line growth was driven primarily by a surge in theatrical system sales (up 133% for the year) and recurring rights for use, rental, and maintenance revenues (up 32%). This performance underscores the effectiveness of D-BOX’s commercial strategy and its ability to scale profitably. D-BOX continued to outperform the North American domestic box office on an annualized basis, with the box office increasing 7%3 while D-BOX royalties rose 32%. Our base of motion-enabled screens grew by 18.7% year-over-year, reaching 1,201 active screens. D-BOX’s technology is increasingly recognized as a premium upgrade by exhibitors, enabling them to maximize per-patron yield and deliver unique, immersive experiences. The expansion of the installed footprint directly supports high-margin, recurring royalty and maintenance revenues, which have proven resilient and outperformed broader box office trends. This continued outperformance reinforces D-BOX’s growing position within the premium theatrical ecosystem and further supports the long-term scalability of its recurring revenue model. Although revenues from sim racing and simulation and training system sales declined by 10% and 16% respectively, these segments continue to play a strategically important role within D-BOX’s broader commercial platform and overall profitability profile. Both customer groups utilize the same core motion technology and components as our theatrical clients, which allows D-BOX to leverage economies of scale across its operations. By sharing operating overhead and production resources, these segments help optimize cost efficiency and enhance profitability, even during periods of lower sales. This synergy not only mitigates risk but also improves operating leverage, enhances production scale efficiencies, and preserves D-BOX’s ability to capitalize on growth opportunities across multiple end markets."}}}, "rest_id": "2061921009972744328", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4132", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2061924590696185880", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2536", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Portfolio Update (June 2026): +158.57% (YTD)

1. $SIVE (40%) - CPO / 1.6T for Photonics

I didn’t touch $SIVE this month. I held my position and let the market do the work. It was a 30% allocation, but after a 75% surge over the last 30 days, it grew to 40%.

The thesis is simple, and my conviction has only deepened over the last few months. People are cheering for $10, but they are missing the bigger picture: we haven’t even seen what 2027 looks like yet. Or 2028! 

There is already structural validation across the board with hyperscalers, OEMs, $AAPL, $AEVA, and US defense supply chains. Add factors like the institutional reports, $GFS tech validation, the upcoming US listing, Chips Act, MOU... 

Almost lost count of how many catalysts we got. 😅

$NVDA needs 20x more InP laser capacity by 2030, and current suppliers can only address 60% of that. Guess what? $SIVE comes to the rescue!

Will look to keep accumulating during decent entries throughout H2'26. 

2. $NBIS (35%) - AI Cloud for Compute

Up from 30% to 35% of my portfolio. This is a long-term compounder. The compute demand is astronomical, and their world-class technology and software integration set them apart. 

Management completely won me over. I see this as a $100B company by year-end. If anyone is positioned to become the 4th major hyperscaler, it is Nebius. 

With major catalysts lined up for H2 2026, selling - or even trimming - would be absolutely criminal in my view.

Also it's Jensen's favorite child. 😆

3. $SMCI & $PENG (10% overall) - AI Servers

Both ride the massive AI server trend (heading to $100 soon), but with distinct technological edges.

$PENG caught my eye because of its immense upside in the memory and photonics sectors, which will take their margins to a completely new level compared to today.

$SMCI is setting up for a $DELL like run. Trading at less than 1x sales? It is a no-brainer. AI server demand is compounding, and only 40% of planned US data centers are currently operational. Once the export-control narrative fades, the upside is massive - especially with Elon (SpaceX, xAI, Tesla) as a client. 

4. $TRT (5%) - Back-end Testing

ATMP is still an underlooked sector in the AI infrastructure buildout with many hidden goldmines. 

$TRT looks great to capitalize on the critical need for chip testing across multiple sectors. When demand vastly outstrips supply, the margin for low-quality chips shrinks to zero.

They are posting triple-digit growth, and margins will aggressively expand through 2027 and 2028 as new orders flood in. 

The current upside is driven by CPU/GPUs (with $AMD as the likely customer) and Power Semi, but I see them expanding into Memory and Photonics as well. 

Capacity expansion is a massive bullish signal, and their balance sheet is pristine (0.1 D/E). 

I see this easily breaking $50 by the end of the year, or H1 2027 at the latest.

5. $AAOI & $MU (5%)

$MU is a multi-month swing trade for me, riding the road to a $2T/$3T valuation backed by the American Dream memory narrative.

With $AAOI, I am still evaluating. I might swing it to $300, or I might accumulate more as a long position, because this definitely looks like a $750 stock by 2028. 

Both are tied to massive, sector-specific demand ramps, with $AAOI in transceiver sales and $MU with HBM. 

6. Cash (5%)

I am actually proud of maintaining a decent cash position. Had I not bought $MU, $AAOI, $SMCI, it would have been around 10%. 

This is giving me the optionality to buy the dip on my core holdings if they hit key levels, or to execute new swing setups when I see the right entry.

Things I did this month:

> Sold $KRKNF & $ZETA: Exited these positions to reallocate into higher-conviction plays within the core AI Infrastructure theme. Both remain great longs in my view, but this was purely a pivot for better short-term upside.

> Locked in some massive gains on $SOI, $AAOI, and $TSEM. However, I ended up getting back into $AAOI after the market handed me a $150 entry - an absolute gift.

> $SOI, $AAOI and $TSEM had also given me huge gains and so I decided to sell them. Bought $AAOI again because of the $150 entry, which was a gift. 

Looking ahead: 

> Now that I got memory exposure (last month's goal), my research is shifting toward Energy and Power Semi names. Looking to potentially add one or two high-conviction companies here next.

> I’m intentionally sitting out of Space stocks for now. While I wanted exposure last month, spreading myself too thin risks me loosing focus. Definitely don't want that. 

Make sure you keep doing your own research and building conviction. We already have many soft hands in this market. You don't need to be another one!

- AI

text:
Wall Street is obsessed with AI chips.
But some investors are already looking at the next bottleneck.
@MichelleMakori explains – photonics uses light instead of electricity to move data between processors and servers. As AI demand explodes and copper constraints emerge, some believe optical networking could become critical infrastructure for the AI economy.
Is this where the next opportunity lies?
@MikeLeeStrategy weighs in on The Real Story with Michelle Makori.
Watch the full episode here: 
https://t.co/hUi9BTlEd6

Congratulations 🎉 to the winners of the 2026 Optica awards and medals! 

Find the full list of recipients in the OPN June issue: https://t.co/fiqyj8ejir

#honors #awards #optics #photonics https://t.co/qnI7q59Bcb

NTT is setting up a $440M+ fund to build next-gen optical AI infrastructure. Partners: SK Group (Korea) and Chunghwa Telecom (Taiwan).

The tech behind it is called IOWN. All-photonics network. No electrical signal conversion. Lower latency, lower power draw, higher bandwidth. NTT and Chunghwa already have a working link between Taipei and Tokyo running at 100Gbps with 34ms round-trip.

This is three countries pooling money to build AI infrastructure outside the US cloud stack. Most AI compute runs through AWS, Azure, and GCP. Asia is wiring its own backbone.

The real question: does AI's future run on American clouds or Asian light?

My favourite asymmetries right now:  

> $AAOI - Raised FY26 guidance to $1.1B+ with demand outrunning supply into mid-2027 on the 800G and 1.6T ramp, then sold off 12% on the print. A raised guide into a sell-off is the setup.

>  $MRVL - Jensen called it the next trillion dollar company at Computex. $2B Nvidia stake, NVLink Fusion, and a genuine moat in custom silicon and silicon photonics. You can overcomplicate it and pretend you know more than the Godfather of AI, or you can just buy. 

> $KEEL - Amazon hiring in Moses Lake where Keel is building, first hyperscaler lease is the trigger. Panther Creek at 350MW is the real prize. This is just proof of execution. 

> $AMPG - relaunched its website and listed  $AMZN and $NVDA as customers for the first time. The Nvidia link traces to the AI-RAN work with Northeastern. Amazon is brand new. Management has already said new contracts are coming and would be announced soon.

> $NBIS - Do I really need to say anything

text:
$NVDA CEO Basically Claims That the Memory Shortages Aren't Ending Anytime Soon

"The memory market has recently been shrouded in negative rumors that "Nvidia has halved its use of memory modules in Vera Rubin server racks." However, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang came forward to refute the rumors, stating that 'there is no end in sight to the memory shortage," and announced that the company will purchase more memory from SK Hynix. The two companies will also work together to develop next-generation memory technology.

Analysts point out that Huang's remarks dispelled rumors of a sharp decline in Nvidia's memory usage, providing reassurance to memory manufacturers such as Nanya Technology (2408) and Winbond (2344) regarding their future prospects. Nvidia's plan to purchase more SK Hynix high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and collaborate on next-generation technology development will likely benefit Taiwanese manufacturers, particularly TSMC's subsidiary, Creative Labs (3443), which is working with SK Hynix to further develop HBM4-related ASIC-side IP.

Huang pointed out that the current demand in the AI ​​industry far exceeds the supply. The supply chain, from wafer manufacturing and advanced packaging to silicon photonics technology, is in a state of supply shortage. The demand is so large that it is difficult to meet. He predicts that this situation will continue for many years. In addition, AI infrastructure is still in a stage of rapid expansion, and there are no signs of the global data center investment boom cooling down.

When asked about the rumor that "Nvidia's Vera Rubin server rack memory module usage has been halved," Huang refuted it, emphasizing that "there is no end in sight to the memory shortage."

text:
LPKF Laser & Electronics SE (XETRA Germany: LPK) . They are a highly specialized German high-tech engineering and photonics company that builds precision, laser-based manufacturing systems.

Essentially, they create the complex laser machines that other companies use to build microchips, solar panels, and circuit boards.
The Quick Profile

Headquarters: Garbsen, Germany (near Hanover).
Founded: 1976 (they started out making mechanical tools for prototyping circuit boards before moving heavily into lasers).
Size: Publicly traded on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange, with around 700 to 750 employees globally.

What Do They Actually Make?
Instead of making consumer electronics, LPKF operates behind the scenes in four main industrial sectors:

1/ Advanced Semiconductor Packaging (The Big Growth Area): They are currently making major waves in the microchip industry with a patented technology called LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching). As silicon chips hit their physical limits, the industry is shifting toward using glass as a base (interposer) for next-gen AI and high-performance computing chips. LIDE allows companies to micro-drill and structure glass without causing micro-cracks or defects.

2/ Electronics & Circuit Boards: They make laser depaneling systems (glorified, ultra-precise laser cutters) that slice fragile, flexible printed circuit boards (PCBs) out of larger sheets during manufacturing without damaging the tiny components. They also sell "Rapid Prototyping" benchtop lasers to universities and research labs so engineers can print custom circuit boards in-house.
3/ Laser Plastic Welding: They build machines that use lasers to fuse plastic parts together seamlessly. This is huge in medical devices and automotive manufacturing (like EV cooling systems)—especially with their recent "Absorbing-to-Absorbing" technology, which allows two dark, laser-absorbing plastics to be welded together without needing a transparent layer.
4/ Solar Energy: They develop laser scribing systems used to score and structure thin-film solar cells, increasing their efficiency. They are currently pivoting these machines to support next-generation perovskite solar cells.
1. Core Physics of the Disruption (LIDE® Technology)As an AI semiconductor specialist and physicist, the document analyzes the fundamental breakdown of organic packaging substrates when handling multi-GHz signals required by modern LLM compute clusters.The Problem: Conventional substrate materials experience high dielectric loss and mechanical warpage under the severe thermal fluctuations generated by massive tensor processing units.  The Solution: Moving to a glass core substrate. LPKF owns a patented chemical-physical process called LIDE® (Laser-Induced Deep Etching).  The Physics: The system utilizes precise, multi-photon laser absorption to alter the chemical microstructure of technical glasses (such as borosilicate) locally without injecting thermal shock or micro-cracks into the surrounding matrix. A subsequent selective wet-etching process attacks the modified glass at an accelerated selectivity ratio of up to 1:50.

I rode $iren and $corz with you back in the corzq days.

I’m Looking to keep the beach house and aim for the ranch - I landed on photonics as a replacement for copper in data centers going forward and upgrades to exsisting.

Debating between $KOPN (1 bill MC) who is integrated into dept of war w/ Anduril domestic production and is breaking into data centers with Micro LED - with gpu to gpu communication vs one of the larger established but not American made and 100B market cap 

Any thoughts this small asymmetric play or overall theme?

Portfolio update week 19 - Road to 100K💰 

Balance: £14,150.01 💸 

YTD  - 24.4% 😢 

Current Positions ⬇️

$IREN - 300 Shares 

$MEMORYETF - £413.44

$PHOTONICSETF - £297.22

$NBIS - 1.055 Shares 

Big pullback on Friday but the port has responded well, $IREN back on its way to $60 is great to see 

I added £600 into the portfolio and started 3 new positions. I’ve created a memory and photonics pie that I plan to DCA into over the next few months as those are the 2 sectors along with DCs I am most bullish on 

I’ve also started a position in $NBIS however I am unsure if I am going to continue building it out or if I should just focus on the 2 pies I have built 🤔

text:
📊 Top stocks on LunarCrush right now: who's moving and why.

These are ranked by LunarCrush AltRank, which measures social attention and market performance across the entire stock market. The data breaks into three groups today. 

1️⃣ AI, space, and defense names with real catalysts 
2️⃣ Biotech and health plays moving on deals and data 
3️⃣ A name-mixup trap inflating one struggling stock

Reddit $RDDT +8.92% 
Sits at #1. People are talking up its AI data-licensing deals as a real money source, pushing its share of the stock conversation to almost 5x its normal level. Sentiment is only 62% though, with analyst downgrades pushing back.

GRAIL $GRAL +10.15% 
The cancer-screening company caught a bid on partnership rumors ahead of new trial data. One caveat: a lot of the "Grail" chatter is actually about fashion resale and Pokemon "grails," so the real signal is smaller than the number looks.

Alight $ALIT +8.38% 
Be careful with this one. Almost all the buzz is about Alight Motion, a video-editing app, not the actual HR company. The stock itself is down on the week with delisting rumors hanging over it, so the high rank is mostly a name mix-up.

Abivax $ABVX +16.72% 
This biotech popped on takeover rumors with key trial data due in Q2. Mentions and engagement hit all-time highs, but the move is speculation-driven, and fears of the company raising money are capping how far bulls will chase it.

Redwire $RDW +17.59%

Oscar Health $OSCR +16.34%

BlackBerry $BB +6.66% 
The old phone brand is now an AI-software story. A new stock buyback and its QNX car-software business sent it to fresh highs, up about 80% on the month. Some doubters still bring up its decline years.

Ouster $OUST +7.93% 
A LiDAR and robotics-sensing company that broke out on a new product line called Rev8 and a FieldAI partnership. It's up about 118% on the month at 84% sentiment, riding the robots-and-automation theme.

text:
The recent buzz surrounding Marvell Technology $MRVL becoming a potential trillion-dollar company stems largely from a high-profile endorsement by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang at the Computex 2026 trade show. The analysts and industry observers point to several fundamental pillars that support this ambitious valuation thesis:
1 Essential Data Center Connectivity: As AI workloads become disaggregated and distributed across thousands of chips, connectivity has become the primary bottleneck. Marvell provides the essential networking and connectivity chips that allow these massive clusters to communicate at the high speeds required for modern AI.  

2 Strategic Nvidia Partnership: Nvidia, which has a deep interest in the success of the AI ecosystem, recently invested $2 billion in $MRVL. This partnership, centered on NVLink Fusion, aims to integrate their technologies to build more efficient AI infrastructure, essentially making Marvell a critical partner rather than just a vendor.  

3 Leadership in Custom Silicon (ASICs): Marvell is a leader in custom-designed chips for major hyperscalers like Amazon and Microsoft. Unlike general-purpose GPUs, these ASICs are tailored for specific tasks, creating sticky, multi-year revenue streams that provide a massive, predictable growth foundation.  

4 Optical Networking Expertise: Marvell is at the forefront of the shift toward optical interconnects, which use light instead of electricity to move data. This technology is critical for scaling AI performance as bandwidth demands continue to explode.  

5 Industry-Leading Product Innovation: The company recently launched the Teralynx T100, an industry-first 102.4 Tbps switch designed specifically to address latency and energy constraints in AI data centers, keeping them ahead of competitors.  

6 Rapidly Expanding AI Infrastructure Revenue: Data center operations now drive roughly 74% of Marvell's total revenue. The company has projected strong, sustained double-digit growth in this segment, fueled by the global buildout of AI data centers.  

7 Aggressive M&A Strategy: Marvell has been strategically acquiring startups like Celestial AI and XConn to bolster its capabilities in advanced packaging and optical interconnects, allowing it to quickly fill gaps in its technological roadmap.  

8 Silicon Photonics Potential: Marvell is heavily investing in silicon photonics, a technology that promises to significantly improve the energy efficiency and data transfer speeds of future AI clusters. This is widely considered a "real" long-term growth driver.

9 One-Stop Shop Positioning: By offering a comprehensive suite of solutions, from processors and ASICs to optical I/O and switching. Marvell is positioning itself as a vital, end-to-end infrastructure provider for the cloud-scale AI era.  

10 Market Momentum and Valuation Re-rating: Following Jensen Huang's endorsement, the market has begun to aggressively re-price Marvell, reflecting the expectation that it will capture a massive share of the AI hardware market in the coming years. This investor confidence acts as a catalyst for the capital influx needed to support a larger market cap.

text:
$MRVL Marvell Technology: Architect of the AI Connectivity Backbone. Investment Thesis.

Marvell Technology has completed a strategic transformation that few legacy semiconductor companies execute cleanly. The pivot away from traditional networking and storage semiconductors toward custom silicon and high-speed connectivity for AI data center infrastructure is not a repositioning in progress — it's largely done, and the financial trajectory is beginning to reflect it. The Celestial AI acquisition extends that positioning into optical interconnects and photonic fabrics, addressing the next layer of AI infrastructure bottleneck where copper interconnects hit physical limits at scale.

The hyperscaler demand dynamic is the engine behind the revenue ramp thesis. Custom ASIC design wins with major cloud providers represent a different category of customer relationship than merchant silicon sales — they create deep technical integration, long program lifecycles, and switching costs that are measured in years of co-development rather than procurement cycles. That stickiness is a genuine competitive moat, but it also concentrates revenue in ways that make the business sensitive to program-level decisions at a small number of accounts.

Matthew Murphy's leadership has been the consistent thread through the transformation. The strategic decisions — which legacy businesses to exit, which adjacencies to pursue, which acquisitions to make — reflect a coherent vision that has been executed with unusual discipline for a company managing simultaneous portfolio transitions. That track record matters when evaluating whether the optical interconnect bet will be executed with similar precision.

Technical execution at advanced nodes is the capability risk that the strategic narrative doesn't eliminate. Custom silicon at leading-edge process nodes requires sustained investment in design talent, EDA infrastructure, and customer co-development resources. The competitive environment for that talent — against hyperscalers building internal silicon teams and peers targeting the same design win opportunities — is intensifying, which adds cost pressure alongside the execution complexity.

The valuation is the honest tension in the thesis. Marvell is priced as a confirmed AI infrastructure compounder, and the revenue ramp forecasts embedded in the current multiple require hyperscaler demand to remain elevated, program ramps to execute on schedule, and optical interconnect adoption to accelerate at the pace the Celestial AI acquisition assumes. Each of those is plausible individually — together, they represent a high bar that leaves the stock exposed to any single point of execution friction. The strategic positioning is among the strongest in the AI semiconductor supply chain; the question is how much of that quality is already in the price.

Investment Thesis. Marvell Technology has completed a strategic transformation that few legacy semiconductor companies execute cleanly. The pivot away from traditional networking and storage semiconductors toward custom silicon and high-speed connectivity for AI data center infrastructure is not a repositioning in progress — it's largely done, and the financial trajectory is beginning to reflect it. The Celestial AI acquisition extends that positioning into optical interconnects and photonic fabrics, addressing the next layer of AI infrastructure bottleneck where copper interconnects hit physical limits at scale. The hyperscaler demand dynamic is the engine behind the revenue ramp thesis. Custom ASIC design wins with major cloud providers represent a different category of customer relationship than merchant silicon sales — they create deep technical integration, long program lifecycles, and switching costs that are measured in years of co-development rather than procurement cycles. That stickiness is a genuine competitive moat, but it also concentrates revenue in ways that make the business sensitive to program-level decisions at a small number of accounts. Matthew Murphy's leadership has been the consistent thread through the transformation. The strategic decisions — which legacy businesses to exit, which adjacencies to pursue, which acquisitions to make — reflect a coherent vision that has been executed with unusual discipline for a company managing simultaneous portfolio transitions. That track record matters when evaluating whether the optical interconnect bet will be executed with similar precision. Technical execution at advanced nodes is the capability risk that the strategic narrative doesn't eliminate. Custom silicon at leading-edge process nodes requires sustained investment in design talent, EDA infrastructure, and customer co-development resources. The competitive environment for that talent — against hyperscalers building internal silicon teams and peers targeting the same design win opportunities — is intensifying, which adds cost pressure alongside the execution complexity. The valuation is the honest tension in the thesis. Marvell is priced as a confirmed AI infrastructure compounder, and the revenue ramp forecasts embedded in the current multiple require hyperscaler demand to remain elevated, program ramps to execute on schedule, and optical interconnect adoption to accelerate at the pace the Celestial AI acquisition assumes. Each of those is plausible individually — together, they represent a high bar that leaves the stock exposed to any single point of execution friction. The strategic positioning is among the strongest in the AI semiconductor supply chain; the question is how much of that quality is already in the price."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "article": {"article_results": {"result": {"cover_media": {"id": "QXBpTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHJ/ZrKgWgAAKAAIZgoybDlbAAAAA", "media_id": "2062606489936625664", "media_info": {"__typename": "ApiImage", "color_info": {"palette": [{"percentage": 88.28, "rgb": {"blue": 254, "green": 254, "red": 254}}, {"percentage": 9.33, "rgb": {"blue": 43, "green": 44, "red": 42}}]}, "original_img_height": 778, "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HJ_ZrKgWgAAZhaU.jpg", "original_img_width": 1945}, "media_key": "3_2062606489936625664"}, "id": "QXJ0aWNsZUVudGl0eToyMDYyNjA1NDU4MDI4ODA2MTQ1", "lifecycle_state": {"modified_at_secs": 1780602097}, "metadata": {"first_published_at_secs": 1780602097}, "preview_text": "EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW: Marvell Technology is now one of the most direct public-market vehicles for the AI data-center connectivity bottleneck. We are hyped to the sky. LLMs are stochastic parrots and Governance/Risk/Compliance (GRC) is currently ripping them through. 

- AI is deflationary. Deflationary tech needs inflationary money.

- BTC is “cash-equivalent” in a portfolio comprising of growth assets. There will be hype assets (all non-dividend paying assets are hype assets by definition) that will return more than BTC in short term

- AI bubble will pop. Infra/photonics/energy buildout will remain to power the next AI wave.

text:
$CRDO Fundamental Deep Dive 🟢 Credo Tech

OVERVIEW

Credo operates as a hyper-growth high-speed connectivity semiconductor provider that has a broad portfolio of products spanning SerDes, optical DSPs, Active Electrical Cables (AECs), and silicon photonics solutions. FY2025 proved their explosive growth narrative, achieving an annual revenue of $1.34B, growing +206% YoY. The AI/HPC data center segment delivered the bulk of growth, driven by hyperscaler demand, AI cluster infrastructure, and optical/electrical interconnects (800G/1.6T).

$CRDO is the dominant force in delivering copper architecture through its market-leading AECs. Low-power, high-reliability copper cables that facilitate short-reach intra-rack and spine connections in AI clusters today. Over the coming years, the industry will transition toward photonics for longer-reach, higher-density interconnects at 1.6T+ speeds, where copper hits physical limits. The fear with this development was that Credo would be essentially phased out of the market with time, leading to the massive correction in early 2026.

However, the recent DustPhotonics acquisition (completed in May 2026) brings in-house silicon photonics PIC technology for 800G/1.6T/3.2T+ NPO and CPO, solidifying its future fully vertically integrated stack (SerDes + DSP + silicon photonics + system integration). This positions Credo perfectly for the next decade of AI infrastructure evolution, capturing copper today and optics tomorrow.

In my eyes, This company is a premier "picks-and-shovels" play on the connectivity buildout. Margins are disgustingly high (60%+ gross) now and in the years moving forward with a strong FCF + balance sheet, providing the company a leg-up on competition due to minimal capital intensity given their fabless model. These margins are expected to be sustained or even expand further as higher-ASP optical and silicon photonics products ramp.

--------------------------

GROWTH OUTLOOK

$CRDO has consistently outperformed expectations without sandbagging guidance. Management projects robust revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, driven largely by AI connectivity + photonics acceleration.

Credo maintains deep partnerships with all major hyperscalers (including $MSFT, $AMZN, $META, $ORCL and xAI), a fact known due to their famous "purple cables" acting as visual proof across imaged AI/HPC builds. Their solutions are currently powering xAI’s Colossus cluster via ZeroFlap AECs to maximize GPU utilization and cluster uptime. This is the part many sleep on! These existing relationships give Credo massive leverage to drive future growth when hyperscalers are primed to adopt the new 1.6T+ optical DSPs and silicon photonics products as the transition accelerates.

The AI connectivity segment projects strong growth of ~80%+ YoY in FY26 (with a re-inflection in this strength in the back half of 2027) due to expanded hyperscaler/AI focus. Over FY2026, the company is projected to report ~$2.4B Revenue and ~$6 EPS. I positioned before this inflection starts in March. I am thankful for the opportunity we were provided.

This growth fuels income statement expansion, with non-GAAP gross margins holding steady in the mid-to-high 60s% and operating margins nearing 45-50% as higher-margin optical and photonics products enter the mix further down the line, driving EPS sharply higher. The balance sheet is pristine, currently holding $1.4B in cash with strong FCF generation and zero debt thanks to the fabless model. Product roadmaps support their ambitious outlook, as shown by their unveiling of 1.6T optical DSPs and silicon photonics integration becoming available with next-generation AI programs such as 800G acceleration ramping in 2H 2026. 

--------------------------

FAIR VALUATION ESTIMATE

Using an aggregated/blended valuation approach based on many different disciplines of FA (DCF, Relative Multiples, Analyst Coverage, etcetera)...

FVE = $260/share

This does not take into account the potential for technical momentum to accelerate price far beyond this FVE due to future prospects. One of my favorite tidbits from an FA standpoint for this company is that their NTM P/E is 36.7 and PEG sits at 0.2. This is EXTREMELY LOW in my opinion for the growth promised. Tons of room higher from a valuation standpoint relative to other companies.

--------------------------

LONG-TERM ESTIMATES

Assuming $CRDO meets aggressive growth targets and achieves success in mixing CPO + NPO products into their portfolio with clear customer adoption, this is their potential path forward valuation-wise when using P/E multiples and expected EPS values.

EY2026 = $350+
EY2027 = $500+
EY2028 = $700+
EY2029 = $900+

One of my favorite longs at this time. There is risk involved regarding the integration of DustPhotonics post-acquisition, but I very much so trust management considering their track record.

Credit to @damnang2 for the image breaking down their product portfolio.

4672, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 4679}, {"from_index": 4687, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 4693}]}, "text": "$CRDO Fundamental Deep Dive 🟢 Credo Tech

OVERVIEW

Credo operates as a hyper-growth high-speed connectivity semiconductor provider that has a broad portfolio of products spanning SerDes, optical DSPs, Active Electrical Cables (AECs), and silicon photonics solutions. FY2025 proved their explosive growth narrative, achieving an annual revenue of $1.34B, growing +206% YoY. The AI/HPC data center segment delivered the bulk of growth, driven by hyperscaler demand, AI cluster infrastructure, and optical/electrical interconnects (800G/1.6T).

$CRDO is the dominant force in delivering copper architecture through its market-leading AECs. Low-power, high-reliability copper cables that facilitate short-reach intra-rack and spine connections in AI clusters today. Over the coming years, the industry will transition toward photonics for longer-reach, higher-density interconnects at 1.6T+ speeds, where copper hits physical limits. The fear with this development was that Credo would be essentially phased out of the market with time, leading to the massive correction in early 2026.

However, the recent DustPhotonics acquisition (completed in May 2026) brings in-house silicon photonics PIC technology for 800G/1.6T/3.2T+ NPO and CPO, solidifying its future fully vertically integrated stack (SerDes + DSP + silicon photonics + system integration). This positions Credo perfectly for the next decade of AI infrastructure evolution, capturing copper today and optics tomorrow.

In my eyes, This company is a premier "picks-and-shovels" play on the connectivity buildout. Margins are disgustingly high (60%+ gross) now and in the years moving forward with a strong FCF + balance sheet, providing the company a leg-up on competition due to minimal capital intensity given their fabless model. These margins are expected to be sustained or even expand further as higher-ASP optical and silicon photonics products ramp.

--------------------------

GROWTH OUTLOOK

$CRDO has consistently outperformed expectations without sandbagging guidance. Management projects robust revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, driven largely by AI connectivity + photonics acceleration.

Credo maintains deep partnerships with all major hyperscalers (including $MSFT, $AMZN, $META, $ORCL and xAI), a fact known due to their famous "purple cables" acting as visual proof across imaged AI/HPC builds. Their solutions are currently powering xAI’s Colossus cluster via ZeroFlap AECs to maximize GPU utilization and cluster uptime. This is the part many sleep on! These existing relationships give Credo massive leverage to drive future growth when hyperscalers are primed to adopt the new 1.6T+ optical DSPs and silicon photonics products as the transition accelerates.

The AI connectivity segment projects strong growth of ~80%+ YoY in FY26 (with a re-inflection in this strength in the back half of 2027) due to expanded hyperscaler/AI focus. Over FY2026, the company is projected to report ~$2.4B Revenue and ~$6 EPS. I positioned before this inflection starts in March. I am thankful for the opportunity we were provided.

This growth fuels income statement expansion, with non-GAAP gross margins holding steady in the mid-to-high 60s% and operating margins nearing 45-50% as higher-margin optical and photonics products enter the mix further down the line, driving EPS sharply higher. The balance sheet is pristine, currently holding $1.4B in cash with strong FCF generation and zero debt thanks to the fabless model. Product roadmaps support their ambitious outlook, as shown by their unveiling of 1.6T optical DSPs and silicon photonics integration becoming available with next-generation AI programs such as 800G acceleration ramping in 2H 2026. 

--------------------------

FAIR VALUATION ESTIMATE

Using an aggregated/blended valuation approach based on many different disciplines of FA (DCF, Relative Multiples, Analyst Coverage, etcetera)...

FVE = $260/share

This does not take into account the potential for technical momentum to accelerate price far beyond this FVE due to future prospects. One of my favorite tidbits from an FA standpoint for this company is that their NTM P/E is 36.7 and PEG sits at 0.2. This is EXTREMELY LOW in my opinion for the growth promised. Tons of room higher from a valuation standpoint relative to other companies.

--------------------------

LONG-TERM ESTIMATES

Assuming $CRDO meets aggressive growth targets and achieves success in mixing CPO + NPO products into their portfolio with clear customer adoption, this is their potential path forward valuation-wise when using P/E multiples and expected EPS values.

EY2026 = $350+
EY2027 = $500+
EY2028 = $700+
EY2029 = $900+

One of my favorite longs at this time. There is risk involved regarding the integration of DustPhotonics post-acquisition, but I very much so trust management considering their track record.

Credit to @damnang2 for the image breaking down their product portfolio."}}}, "rest_id": "2062621942885331091", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2665", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Those companies with access to the most power will rule the world.

text: After researching optical photonics for several weeks & learning how to build systems from basic components, I finally bought the highly valuable system that we needed to build our 1st solid-state battery prototype. It should be complete by this August. https://t.co/u3KnRGC16b

Nvidia's AI roadmap is becoming a networking story as much as a GPU story. As AI factories grow to millions of GPUs, co-packaged optics and photonics are moving from a niche technology to a critical piece of next-generation infrastructure.

$NVDA $SPY

text:
Two Deep-Tech Innovators Presenting June 10: BIO-key International and Syntec Optics

Two very different technologies — biometric identity authentication and precision photonics — united by one theme: solving problems that require extreme accuracy in high-stakes environments.

BIO-key International (OTC: $BKYI) | 3:00 PM ET - A global leader in Identity and Access Management (IAM) and biometric authentication, serving defense, financial, and government customers across multiple continents.

Syntec Optics Holdings (Nasdaq: $OPTX) | 3:30 PM ET - One of the largest custom optics and photonics manufacturers in the United States, operating for over two decades out of its Rochester, NY facility.

Two companies. Two distinct markets. Both operating at the leading edge of their fields.

June 10, 2026 | Register free: https://t.co/F6isjDz8Ci
